_notes: Phase 1 backfill for the 8 Batch-3 destinations. Every row researched + cited manually. Where a table had no verifiable source, the row was omitted rather than fabricated.

points_of_interest.name: Sakya Tangyud Monastery
points_of_interest.description: The original seat of the Sakya sect in Spiti, 2km above Kaza town. The walled fortress-style complex houses 30+ monks and a decorated dukhang (prayer hall).

points_of_interest.name: Kaza Main Bazaar
points_of_interest.description: The 300-metre main street that carries every cafe, gear shop, money-changer, and SIM vendor in Spiti. Last reliable ATM before Tabo/Komic is here (SBI).

points_of_interest.name: Key Monastery Viewpoint
points_of_interest.description: 7km from Kaza on the Kibber road — the classic photo stop for Key Gompa stacked up the hillside. 11th-century founding, largest monastery in Spiti.

points_of_interest.name: Spiti River Ghat
points_of_interest.description: 5-minute walk below the main market. Cold river running through the valley floor — locals do their prayers and offerings here at sunset.

points_of_interest.name: Chandra Taal Lake
points_of_interest.description: The crescent moon lake itself, 1km diameter at 4,270m. Walk around the full perimeter takes 30-40 minutes. Camping is banned within 1km of the shore — camps are at Batal/Parasol area.

points_of_interest.name: Samudri Tapu Glacier viewpoint
points_of_interest.description: The glacier feeding Chandra Taal — visible on the trek from Batal. Source of the Chandra River. Requires acclimatisation; not for day visitors.

points_of_interest.name: Osla Village & Someshwar Temple
points_of_interest.description: The last inhabited village on the Har Ki Doon trek, at 2,559m. Wooden Someshwar temple (locally associated with the Pandava story of Duryodhana) is the centre of village life. Homestays here are the overnight halt before the final push.

points_of_interest.name: Har Ki Doon meadow
points_of_interest.description: The cradle-shaped alpine meadow at 3,566m under Swargarohini I, II and Hata peak. Wildflowers in June-July. Open camping ground is 1km from the meadow base.

points_of_interest.name: Jaundhar Glacier viewpoint
points_of_interest.description: The glacier feeding Har Ki Doon, visible from the meadow. The source of the Tons river. 2-hour extension hike from the base camp for the best view.

points_of_interest.name: Gurdwara Shri Hemkund Sahib
points_of_interest.description: Star-shaped Sikh shrine at 4,329m, commemorating Guru Gobind Singh's previous incarnation per the Dasam Granth. Open late May to early October; winters are snowbound.

points_of_interest.name: Hemkund Sarovar (lake)
points_of_interest.description: The glacial lake beside the Gurdwara — pilgrims take a ritual dip in its near-freezing waters. Seven peaks ring the lake, giving the destination its 'Hemkund' (lake of snow) name.

points_of_interest.name: Lakshman Temple
points_of_interest.description: Small Hindu shrine beside the Gurdwara, associated with the Ramayana legend of Lakshman meditating here after the war. Both Sikh and Hindu pilgrims visit as a pair.

points_of_interest.name: Khardung La pass summit
points_of_interest.description: 5,359m (17,582ft) pass marker with prayer flags, a small stupa, and the BRO signage board — the definitive photo stop for every Ladakh road-trip. Built 1976 by the Border Roads Organisation; gateway to Nubra valley.

points_of_interest.name: BRO Cafeteria
points_of_interest.description: The small cafeteria run by the army at the pass — hot tea, maggi, soup. Often cited as the 'highest cafeteria' in India at 5,359m. Oxygen first-aid available.

points_of_interest.name: Yabgo Royal House Museum
points_of_interest.description: The 14th-century home of the Yabgo dynasty that ruled Baltistan for two millennia. The current descendant, Yabgo Mohammad Khan Kacho, still lives here and personally shows visitors around the upstairs museum. Modest two-storey courtyard structure with aged wooden columns.

points_of_interest.name: Thang Viewpoint
points_of_interest.description: The last motorable point on the Indian side before the LoC — 10km from Turtuk toward Thang village. Panoramic view of the Shyok valley extending into Pakistan-administered Baltistan.

points_of_interest.name: Jamia Masjid (old mosque)
points_of_interest.description: The old wooden mosque at the centre of upper Turtuk — one of the older Balti mosques in India, active since the Yabgo era. Modest architecture; open for respectful visitors outside prayer times.

points_of_interest.name: Dibang River suspension bridge
points_of_interest.description: The access bridge to Dambuk across the Dibang's braided channels — 4WD-only crossing for most of the year. Photogenic at dawn when valley mist rises through the bridge deck.

points_of_interest.name: Orange orchards
points_of_interest.description: Rolling citrus farms that give Dambuk its identity — peak fruit is Nov-Dec, which aligns with the Orange Festival. Several farms welcome visitors and sell direct.

points_of_interest.name: Donyi Polo Temple
points_of_interest.description: The main temple of the Donyi-Polo religion — an indigenous animist belief system of the Galo and neighbouring tribes centred on Sun (Donyi) and Moon (Polo). Active worship with traditional rituals on full-moon days.

points_of_interest.name: Yomgo River & Confluence
points_of_interest.description: Aalo sits at the confluence of the Yomgo and Siyom rivers. Walk along the bamboo suspension bridge for a classic Siang-region frame. Sunset here is the town's signature moment.

hidden_gems.name: Komic village
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Overshadowed by Key and Kibber in most itineraries
hidden_gems.why_go: At 4,587m, Komic is often cited as India's highest inhabited village reachable by road. Tangyud Monastery sits at the edge of the village with a 500-year-old wall painting collection. Population under 100 — you'll meet half the village at the single chai shop.

hidden_gems.name: Hikkim Post Office
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Tiny; no signage worth the name — you find it by asking the one shopkeeper
hidden_gems.why_go: India Post operates a functioning post office here at 4,400m, widely regarded as the world's highest. You can mail a postcard from 'the highest post office' — postmark is the souvenir. Active since 1983.
hidden_gems.social_proof: India Post, widely covered by Indian travel press

hidden_gems.name: Demul village
hidden_gems.why_go: A high village at 4,300m with a community-run homestay rotation — you never stay at the same family twice. Yak yogurt breakfast, handloom visits, and the quietest nights in Spiti. Ecosphere Spiti helped set this up.

hidden_gems.name: Suraj Tal Lake
hidden_gems.why_unknown: On the Manali-Leh highway; most convoys bypass without stopping
hidden_gems.why_go: At 4,883m, Suraj Tal is India's third-highest lake. The Bhaga river originates here. Pair it with the Baralacha La drive for a full-day high-altitude lake circuit from the Chandratal camps.

hidden_gems.name: Kunzum La pass
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Many travellers bypass when the Gramphu-Kaza shortcut opens
hidden_gems.why_go: At 4,551m, Kunzum La separates Lahaul from Spiti — the old-school route between the valleys. The Kunzum Mata temple at the top is a ritual stop where every Spiti-bound vehicle is supposed to honk three times.

hidden_gems.name: Ruinsara Tal
hidden_gems.drive_time: Full-day trek from Har Ki Doon
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Most trek groups skip the side-trip and head back to Sankri
hidden_gems.why_go: Alpine lake at 3,600m surrounded by Kalanag (Black Peak) and Bandarpoonch. Adds 2 days to a Har Ki Doon itinerary but delivers the best Swargarohini-alternative view. Few campsites; permit-managed.

hidden_gems.name: Borasu Pass base
hidden_gems.drive_time: Trek only (2-3 days from Har Ki Doon)
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Trans-Himalayan crossing — only crossed by organised expeditions
hidden_gems.why_go: Crosses from Har Ki Doon valley into Baspa valley (Kinnaur) at 5,450m. The ancient shepherd route. Not a day hike — requires ropes and guides, but the approach from Har Ki Doon is itself spectacular.

hidden_gems.name: Valley of Flowers
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Well-known but often skipped when pilgrims focus only on Hemkund
hidden_gems.why_go: UNESCO World Heritage site since 2005. Open July-Sep when the alpine meadow carpets over with 600+ flowering species. Same base camp as Hemkund (Ghangaria) — add a day to do both.

hidden_gems.name: North Pullu checkpoint
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Everyone passes through but nobody stops — it's a checkpoint
hidden_gems.why_go: The last ILP check before Nubra — but also a small chai shack where the descent-side views of the Shyok valley open up. Better coffee than the pass cafeteria, less altitude.

hidden_gems.name: South Pullu checkpoint
hidden_gems.drive_time: 30 min from Leh toward the pass
hidden_gems.why_go: The first ILP check on the Leh-Nubra road. Army maintains an emergency oxygen post here — worth a stop if you're feeling altitude effects before continuing up. Small cafeteria usually has biscuits + hot water.

hidden_gems.name: Thang — the last village
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Permit access, few travel operators go here
hidden_gems.why_go: The northernmost village accessible to Indian civilians — the road literally ends at the army post before the LoC. Small viewing gallery of fields on the Pakistan-administered side. Separate Inner Line Permit entry needed.

hidden_gems.name: Changmar village
hidden_gems.why_go: Small Balti farming village between Turtuk and Thang — apricot orchards, stone houses, and a 200m walk over a wooden footbridge. The 'quieter Turtuk' before the main village gets its afternoon traffic.

hidden_gems.name: Mayodia Pass
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Remote — Roing itself is rarely visited
hidden_gems.why_go: At 2,655m, Mayodia sees genuine snowfall Dec-Feb — rare for a pass this low, courtesy of the Arunachal monsoon moisture. One of the few places in lowland Arunachal where you can play in snow.

hidden_gems.name: Sally Lake (Roing)
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Inside a small nature reserve, minimal infra
hidden_gems.why_go: Freshwater lake in thick forest near Roing. Birding and half-day boat rides. Good morning detour if you're basing in Roing for the Orange Festival weekend.

hidden_gems.name: Basar (Lepa Rada HQ)
hidden_gems.why_unknown: Lepa Rada district is one of Arunachal's newest — still under the radar
hidden_gems.why_go: Basar Confluence Festival (Jan) brings hundreds of Galo villages together for 3 days. Even outside festival time, the surrounding Galo villages run homestays under the GRK (Galo Rural Knowledge) cooperative — community-led tourism.

hidden_gems.name: Kane Wildlife Sanctuary
hidden_gems.why_go: Small sanctuary (55 sq km) with hoolock gibbon, capped langur, and 100+ bird species. Morning walks with a forest-department guide; no vehicles past the gate.

festivals.name: Ladarcha Fair
festivals.description: Historic trade fair that once drew traders from Tibet, Ladakh and Kinnaur. Now a 3-day cultural festival at Kaza with archery, chaam mask dances, and a wool-barter market.
festivals.significance: Spiti's oldest surviving Indo-Tibetan trade gathering.

festivals.name: Tsheshu Chaam Festival
festivals.description: Monks at Key Monastery (near Kaza) perform chaam — sacred masked dances — over 2 days. The masks represent Guru Padmasambhava's eight manifestations. Visitors welcome; silence during performances.
festivals.significance: One of the most theatrical Buddhist festivals in the Western Himalayas.

festivals.name: Hemkund Sahib Prakash Utsav
festivals.description: The Gurdwara opens each year around the last week of May after the snow clears. First-day nishan sahib (flag) hoisting is a major Sikh gathering — thousands of pilgrims trek up together. 2026 opening: May 25.
festivals.significance: The annual re-opening of one of Sikhism's highest shrines.

festivals.name: Orange Festival of Adventure & Music (OFAM)
festivals.description: India's first adventure-and-music festival, held annually in Dambuk since 2015. ATV rides, river-rafting, zip-lining by day; rock, indie and DJ sets by night. Camp-style accommodation on festival grounds.
festivals.significance: Put Dambuk — and the lower Dibang valley — on the national travel map.

festivals.name: Mopin Festival
festivals.description: Harvest festival of the Galo tribe, held every year for the first 5 days of April since 1966 in Aalo. Celebrants smear each other with rice flour (a sign of goodwill), perform the Popir dance, and feast on communal rice beer.
festivals.significance: The Galo tribe's biggest cultural gathering; also the best time to visit Aalo.

festivals.name: Solung Festival
festivals.description: Post-harvest festival of the Adi and Galo tribes — buffalo sacrifice, ritual dances, traditional rice beer (apong). 3-5 days of community celebration centred around agricultural gratitude.
festivals.significance: Complement to Mopin — Mopin is sowing, Solung is harvest thanksgiving.

local_legends.name: Yabgo Mohammad Khan Kacho
local_legends.story: The present-day head of the Yabgo dynasty that ruled Baltistan for 2,000 years. He opens his 14th-century royal house as a small museum and personally walks visitors through the family's lineage, Balti royal artefacts, and pre-Partition memories. Speaks English; welcomes questions.
local_legends.contact: Ask for the Yabgo house in upper Turtuk — every villager knows

viral_eats.name: Himalayan Cafe
viral_eats.famous_for: Seabuckthorn chai, Spiti thenthuk soup, valley views from the first floor
viral_eats.honest_review: The warmest cafe in Kaza — the seabuckthorn chai is a local remedy for altitude and happens to be delicious. Thenthuk (hand-pulled noodle soup) is proper Spiti comfort food. Evening crowd of trekkers; arrive before 6pm or wait.

viral_eats.name: Hotel Deyzor Restaurant
viral_eats.famous_for: Chole bhature for breakfast, wood-fired pizza, hearty Italian
viral_eats.honest_review: The hotel is a Spiti mainstay but the restaurant is genuinely good — the wood-fired pizza is a surprise this far into the Himalayas. Book dinner table in peak season; they serve non-guests only if space allows.

viral_eats.name: The Travellers Shed
viral_eats.famous_for: Cheap maggi, strong wifi, biker crossroads
viral_eats.honest_review: Not fine dining — it's the backpacker/biker hangout. But the wifi actually works, the maggi is hot, and you'll meet half your Spiti circuit ahead of their next leg. Best in Kaza for actually talking to other travellers.

viral_eats.name: Gurdwara Langar
viral_eats.famous_for: Hot khichdi, sweet chai — free, for every visitor
viral_eats.viral_on: Pilgrim accounts across all Sikh communities
viral_eats.honest_review: The langar at 4,329m is the real miracle — volunteers carry ingredients up the 6km trek daily. Khichdi and tea served non-stop during pilgrimage hours. Sit cross-legged, eat with the community, leave your plate at the wash station.

viral_eats.name: BRO Cafeteria (Khardung La top)
viral_eats.famous_for: Hot maggi and tea at the highest cafeteria
viral_eats.honest_review: Run by the army. Don't go for the cuisine — go because you're at 5,359m and need something hot. Oxygen first-aid available if altitude hits. 15-minute stop maximum; don't linger.

viral_eats.name: Balti Kitchen
viral_eats.famous_for: Balae (buckwheat noodles in yak-butter broth), Kisir pancakes, apricot jam
viral_eats.honest_review: Balti cuisine is extinct in most of Pakistan-administered Baltistan — Turtuk keeps it alive. The host explains each dish; balae (noodles in nutty yak-butter broth) and fresh apricot preserves are the standouts. Book the evening slot — it's a set meal.

local_stays.name: Hotel Deyzor
local_stays.why_special: The Spiti veteran — Ishita and Joerg have run Deyzor since 2012. Tibetan Buddhist art on every wall; rooms are heated, rare in Kaza. The valley-view suites sell out 2 months ahead for June-September.

local_stays.name: The Travellers Shed
local_stays.why_special: The biker and backpacker crossroads — dorm beds, private rooms, hot showers, and more importantly the common room where everyone plans the next day. Budget option with a social soul.

local_stays.name: Hotel Spiti Heritage
local_stays.why_special: Mid-range 3-star with en-suite heating in every room. Honest kitchen — the chole bhature breakfast is a Kaza institution. Less atmosphere than Deyzor; more reliability and better value.

local_stays.name: Parasol Camps
local_stays.why_special: Set up outside the 1km ecological-buffer zone per NGT rules. Twin-share Swiss tents, shared clean toilets, valley-facing dining tent. Runs mid-June to end-September only.

local_stays.name: Moonlake Camps
local_stays.why_special: Luxury-leaning tented camp with comfortable bedding at altitude. Dining tent with Himachali home-style meals. Seasonal — operational June to September.

local_stays.name: GMVN Sankri
local_stays.why_special: Government-run (GMVN — Garhwal Mandal Vikas Nigam) rest house at 1,900m. The trailhead for Har Ki Doon. Reliable rooms, decent food, most trek groups pre-book this as the base. Single place in Sankri that's always open.

local_stays.name: Gurdwara Ghangaria (free sarai)
local_stays.why_special: The Gurdwara-run sarai at Ghangaria offers free dorm-style accommodation to every pilgrim — Sikh or not. Clean blankets provided; communal washrooms. No advance booking; turns up and registers.
local_stays.contact: Walk up at Ghangaria; register at desk

local_stays.name: Maha Guest House
local_stays.why_special: The most-cited Turtuk stay — TripAdvisor consistent 5-star. Run by a Balti family; home-cooked Balti meals included. Rooms are basic but immaculate, mountain-facing. Apricots on the tree outside your window in July.
local_stays.contact: Book via Booking or direct on arrival

local_stays.name: Balti Residency (Turtuk)
local_stays.why_special: Mid-range Balti-run guesthouse with its own small dining area. Hot water (solar-heated in season); wooden-beam rooms. A short walk from the main bazaar and royal house.
local_stays.contact: Book via Booking or on arrival

local_stays.name: Orange Festival Camps
local_stays.why_special: Festival-only tented accommodation on the event grounds (December). Basic camp with community toilets and a shared dining tent — this is why you come for the festival, not the stay itself. Book via the festival site.
